mainEntity.name: How much does a Mercedes R280 / R320 CDI engine replacement cost in the UK?

acceptedAnswer.text: A reconditioned R280/R320 CDI engine typically costs £3,000–£5,500 supply only. Fitting adds £1,500–£2,500 depending on location and workshop rates. A typical engine replacement can range from £1,500 to £5,000. Main dealer new engines start from £8,000–£12,000+ supply only. Use our calculator for a figure specific to your car.

mainEntity.name: Is the Mercedes R280 / R320 CDI engine reliable?

acceptedAnswer.text: The OM642 is considered a 'pretty solid' engine. It was the most popular engine choice in the R-Class. However, it has well-documented weaknesses: oil cooler seal leaks (labour-intensive), troublesome swirl flaps, injector seal leaks, and turbo actuator issues. With proper maintenance, these engines can be reliable, but the oil cooler seal issue is a genuine concern.

mainEntity.name: What are the common problems with the Mercedes R280 / R320 CDI?

acceptedAnswer.text: The main known issues are: (1) Oil cooler seal leaks — gaskets can be baked by the engine, causing oil leaks from the V of the engine, (2) Inlet port swirl flap failure — causing loss of power and black smoke, (3) Injector seal leaks and carbon-clogged injectors, (4) Turbocharger and turbo actuator issues, and (5) Crankshaft position sensor failure.

mainEntity.name: Is it worth replacing a Mercedes R280 / R320 CDI engine, or should I scrap the car?

acceptedAnswer.text: Depends on vehicle value and condition. Against a well-kept £5,000–£12,000 R-Class, a £4,000–£5,500 recon is 30–110% of the car's value. For a clean example with full service history and good body condition, replacement is often worth it. However, if the car has other issues (air suspension, rust, electrical faults), evaluate total repair costs before committing.

mainEntity.name: How do I find my Mercedes R280 / R320 CDI engine code?

acceptedAnswer.text: Listed on the VIN plate and vehicle documentation. Both the R280 and R320 CDI use the OM642.950 engine code. The R280 produces 190 hp and 440 Nm; the R320 produces 224 hp and 510 Nm. The R280 was renamed R300 CDI from approximately February 2009. Tell us your registration date and we'll confirm it for free.

mainEntity.name: Can I repair the OM642 oil cooler seal leak, or does it need engine replacement?

acceptedAnswer.text: Yes — the oil cooler seals can be replaced without removing the engine, but the repair is labour-intensive. The oil cooler is located within the V of the engine beneath a host of ancillary parts that must be removed before the cooler can be accessed. The gaskets can be baked by the engine. The repair typically costs £800–£1,500+. If left untreated, oil loss can cause engine damage. At the first sign of oil spots or burning smell, book an inspection immediately.

eraMap.title: Mercedes R280 / R320 CDI — Which Engine Does Your Car Actually Have?

rows.eraNote: 3.0L V6 diesel — 190 hp, 440 Nm. Renamed R300 CDI from 2009
3.0L V6 diesel — 224 hp, 510 Nm. Most popular R-Class variant

replacementCosts.h2: Mercedes R280 / R320 CDI Engine Replacement Costs
replacementCosts.labourEstimate: Fitting an R-Class diesel engine typically takes 14–18 hours at £65–£95/hr (independent) or £110–£160/hr (London/SE dealer). The OM642 oil cooler seal repair is particularly labour-intensive — the cooler is located within the V of the engine, requiring significant disassembly. A reconditioned engine from a specialist typically includes 12-month warranty and nationwide delivery.

cta.label: → Compare R280 / R320 CDI engine prices from UK specialists

problems.title: Oil Cooler Seal Leaks (The Most Labour-Intensive OM642 Fault)
problems.symptoms: Oil spots under the car, burning oil smell from engine bay, low oil level warning, visible oil residue in the V of the engine
problems.repairCost: £800–£1,500+ (oil cooler seal replacement — labour-intensive as the cooler is located within the V of the engine beneath a host of ancillary parts that must be removed before the cooler can be accessed)
problems.replacementCost: £5,500–£8,000 (if ignored — oil loss can cause engine damage)

urgency.label: Check
urgency.text: book inspection urgently if you notice oil spots or burning smell

problems.recommendation: The oil cooler seals on the OM642 V6 engine are a well-documented weakness. When they degrade, they cause oil to leak from the oil cooler, which is located within the V of the engine. The gaskets can be baked by the engine over time. The repair is labour-intensive because the cooler sits beneath a host of ancillary parts that must be removed before it can be accessed — hence the high labour cost. If you notice oil spots or a burning smell, have the oil cooler seals inspected promptly. A £800–£1,500 repair is far cheaper than a £5,500–£8,000 engine replacement.

failureLink.label: Read the full oil cooler seal failure guide

problems.title: Inlet Port Swirl Flap Failure
problems.symptoms: Loss of power, rough running, black smoke from exhaust, engine warning light, rattling from the intake manifold
problems.repairCost: £500–£1,200 (swirl flap repair or intake manifold replacement)

urgency.label: Check
urgency.text: book inspection urgently if you experience power loss or smoke

problems.recommendation: The R320 CDi "isn't without its faults" — the big-selling R320 CDi has "troublesome inlet port swirl flaps". Swirl flap rods can jam or break completely. If you experience loss of power, rough running, or black smoke, have the swirl flap system inspected immediately. A £500–£1,200 repair is far cheaper than the engine damage that can follow if ignored.

failureLink.label: Read the full swirl flap failure guide

problems.title: Injector Seal Leaks & Carbon-Clogged Injectors
problems.symptoms: Rough idle, misfires, hard starting, loss of power, increased fuel consumption, black smoke, engine warning light
problems.replacementCost: N/A — repair only (does not progress to engine replacement if addressed)
problems.recommendation: The R280 CDI and R320 CDI are known for injector seal leaks and carbon-clogged injectors. If you experience rough idle or misfires, have the injectors tested. Catching this early prevents more serious engine damage.

failureLink.label: Read the full injector failure guide

problems.title: Turbocharger & Turbo Actuator Issues
problems.symptoms: Loss of power, whistling or siren-like noise from the engine bay, black smoke, engine warning light

urgency.label: Check
urgency.text: book inspection urgently if you experience power loss or whistling

problems.recommendation: The OM642 is known for turbo and turbo actuator issues. If you experience loss of power or whistling noises, have the turbo system inspected immediately. A £1,200–£2,500 repair is far cheaper than the engine damage that can follow if ignored.

failureLink.label: Read the full turbo failure guide

problems.title: Crankshaft Position Sensor Failure
problems.symptoms: Engine loses power, cranks but won't start, sudden stalling
problems.typicalMileage: Any mileage — a recognised OM642 problem
problems.recommendation: On vehicles with OM642 engines, there is a known issue with a loss of engine power caused by an electrical interruption in the crankshaft sensor. If your engine loses power or won't start, have the crankshaft position sensor checked.

failureLink.label: Read the full sensor failure guide

repairBuyOrReplace.h2: R280 / R320 CDI — Repair, Buy, or Replace?

rows.whenItMakesSense: Labour-intensive but viable on well-maintained examples
Catch early to prevent engine damage
Catch early to prevent engine damage
Full engine replacement is the only reliable fix

buyingChecks.title: Buying This R280 / R320 CDI? Check These First
buyIf: * Full service history with regular oil changes using MB-approved oil
* Documented oil cooler seal replacement — this is the most labour-intensive OM642 fault
* Evidence of regular motorway driving (DPF health)
* No power loss or smoke (swirl flap and turbo health)
* R320 CDi with documented maintenance history
avoidIf: * Oil leaks from the V of the engine — indicates oil cooler seal failure
* Power loss or black smoke — swirl flap or turbo issues
* Poor service history — the OM642 is a good engine but needs proper maintenance
* Check engine light with injector or emission-related codes
* R-Class with air suspension problems — these can be costly

repairBuyOrReplace.closingVerdict: The R280 and R320 CDI both use the OM642 V6 diesel — a "pretty solid" engine that was the most popular engine choice in the R-Class. The R320 produces 224 hp and 510 Nm, making it the more powerful of the two. The OM642 has well-documented weaknesses: oil cooler seal leaks (labour-intensive), troublesome swirl flaps, injector seal leaks, and turbo actuator issues. Buy a well-maintained example with documented service history, and watch for oil leaks and power loss. The R-Class is "a pleasant place to be", but the 7G-Tronic gearbox can be "twitchy" and air suspension issues are common. Without history? Proceed with caution.

cta.label: → Compare R280 / R320 CDI engine prices before you buy

buyingChecklist.intro: Before you buy a used Mercedes R280 or R320 CDI (or before you commit to an engine replacement), check these:
items: * Oil Leaks — Look for oil residue in the V of the engine (oil cooler seal failure)
* Cold Start — Listen for rattling or unusual noises
* Power Delivery — Any loss of power or black smoke? (Swirl flap or turbo issues)
* Injector Health — Any rough idle, misfires, or hard starting?
* Service History — Including the OM642 engine and confirmed oil-interval discipline
* Air Suspension — Check for sagging or compressor noise; suspension repairs are costly
* DPF Health — Check for DPF warning lights; short journeys are a red flag
* Engine Warning Light — Have codes been read? Look for injector, swirl flap or turbo-related codes

engineCodes.matchNote: This table's rows match Section 1B's Era Map exactly.

items.label: * Valvetrain
items.value: DOHC, 4 valves per cylinder, duplex roller chain

items.label: * Fuel System
items.value: Common-rail direct injection, Bosch 3-plunger high-pressure pump
